title: Sudden death of entanglement, rebirth of magic
abstract: Local Markovian noise cannot bring entanglement back, but it can bring magic back. Unlike separability, stabilizer membership is not preserved by local channels, allowing dissipation to push states out of the stabilizer polytope as well as in. Under local amplitude damping, the $n$-qubit GHZ family $α|0^n\rangle+β|1^n\rangle$ ($0<α<β$) loses its magic at a lower damping strength $γ_-$ and regains it at a higher one $γ_+$, while entanglement is irreversibly lost at $γ_e$. This magic-entanglement complementarity, $γ_e+γ_+=1$ for every $n$, reflects a system-environment duality of amplitude damping and persists for a broader class of dissipative channels. For small $α$, the reborn magic resides in a fully separable state with all proper marginals stabilizer, yet parity-syndrome extraction concentrates it onto a single qubit for magic-state distillation. Local dissipation further divides pure stabilizer states into magic-generators and magic-insulators: at two qubits, the Bell state $|Φ^+\rangle$ generates magic immediately, while its Bell-state partner $|Ψ^+\rangle$ remains stabilizer. Together, magic and entanglement reveal a symmetry invisible to either alone.
summary: This paper demonstrates that local amplitude damping can cause the 'sudden death' and subsequent 'rebirth' of magic in quantum states, even after entanglement has been lost. It reveals a fundamental symmetry between entanglement death and magic rebirth and provides a classification of stabilizer states based on their ability to generate magic under noise.
main_problem: Investigating the dynamics of entanglement and magic (non-stabilizerness) under local Markovian amplitude damping, specifically focusing on the phenomenon of magic rebirth after entanglement death.
main_result: The paper establishes that while entanglement is irreversibly lost under amplitude damping, magic can be 'reborn' after an initial period of disappearance, following a complementarity relation gamma_e + gamma_+ = 1. It also classifies stabilizer states into magic-generators and magic-insulators based on their Hamming-weight support.
method: Analytical approach using Kraus operators, Stinespring dilation, and the stabilizer polytope framework, combined with mathematical tools like partial transposition and combinatorial enumeration.
model_or_system: n-qubit systems, specifically the GHZ-type family and Bell states, subject to local Markovian amplitude damping channels.
key_observables: Magic robustness (R), concurrence (C), entanglement negativity, and stabilizer polytope membership.
important_parameters: Damping strength (gamma), number of qubits (n), initial state amplitude ratio (alpha/beta), and phase-twist angle (phi).
main_assumptions: The noise model is restricted to local Markovian amplitude damping (non-unital) and certain phase-covariant channels.
figures_summary: Schematic of trajectories in the stabilizer polytope; plots of magic-entanglement complementarity; visualization of the super-exponential decay of the stabilizer window width; and threshold deformation as a function of phase.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magic vs. entanglement dynamics, analyzes the GHZ-X manifold under amplitude damping, establishes the complementarity relation, classifies stabilizer states into generators and insulators, and explores the asymptotic behavior and phase-twist effects.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into the resource theory of magic and the non-intuitive behavior of quantum resources in open quantum systems, specifically how dissipation can act as a resource for generating non-stabilizer states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Trajectory classes and resource phase diagram. (a) Schematic stabilizer-polytope trajectories under local amplitude damping, all ending at the stabilizer vertex |0n⟩. Red denotes magic death and rebirth, equivalently entry into and exit from S, realized by the GHZ-X family solved in the main text and by an exact non- GHZ-X affine-plane slice in the SM. Blue denotes finite death with- out rebirth, realized by anti-W edges D2 3 and D3 4 . Gray denotes endpoint-only trajectories, with affine examples given by nonstabi- lizer real generalized-W inputs and curved examples such as D2 4

FIG. 2. Magic–entanglement complementarity and Bell-state splitting. (a) Family-A trajectory in Regime II (α = 0.40). Magic (blue) dies at γ(2) − and is reborn at γ(2) + , after entanglement (red) vanishes at γ(2) e . Gray shading marks the stabilizer window. (b) Trajectory-level complementarity for the same parameters as in panel (a): RA(γ) −1 (blue line) coincides with the damping- reflected concurrence 1−γ

FIG. 3. Single-qubit extraction and distillation windows. De- coded trajectories ˜ρ(γ) at α = 0.20 for n = 2, 4, 6 in the Bloch XZ plane. The rotated square is the stabilizer octahedron O. Each trajec- tory enters O at γ(n) − and exits at γ(n) + (labels shown for n = 4). Pink regions mark Bravyi–Kitaev sufficient distillation windows [14]: |H⟩-type x + |z| ≳1.015 (light), and after Clifford twirling |T⟩- type x + |z| > 3/ √

FIG. 4. Super-exponential decay of the stabilizer window. Exact window width ∆n = γ(n) + −γ(n) − (solid lines with markers) against the analytical upper bound (C1) (dotted) for three initial amplitudes spanning the re-entrant regime. At all three amplitudes ∆n decays faster than any exponential in n, and the bound saturates the exact width within ∼10% for n ≳6.
